name: Negation (nai and masen)
summary: Make verbs negative with polite -ません (masen) or plain -ない (nai). Both turn 'do' into 'do not'.
explanation:
To say you do not do something, the polite negative simply changes ます to ません. 食べます (tabemasu, eat) becomes 食べません (tabemasen, do not eat); 行きます becomes 行きません (ikimasen, do not go). This is the safest negative for learners and works everywhere.

The plain negative, the -ない (nai) form, is used in casual speech and many grammar structures. Ichidan verbs drop る and add ない: 食べる becomes 食べない (tabenai). Godan verbs change the final -u to the -a row and add ない: 飲む becomes 飲まない (nomanai), 行く becomes 行かない (ikanai). The two irregulars are する - しない (shinai) and 来る - 来ない (konai).

One special case: the godan verbs ending in う, like 買う (kau), become 買わない (kawanai), inserting わ rather than あ. And the verb ある (aru, to exist for things) has the irregular negative ない. With these few rules, you can negate almost any verb.
pattern: [verb stem] + ません (polite)  /  plain -ない form

conjugation.polite negative: 食べません (tabemasen) - do not eat
conjugation.plain negative (ichidan): 食べない (tabenai) - do not eat
conjugation.plain negative (godan -mu): 飲まない (nomanai) - do not drink
conjugation.plain negative (godan -ku): 行かない (ikanai) - do not go

examples.native: 私は肉を食べません。(watashi wa niku o tabemasen.)
今日は仕事に行きません。(kyou wa shigoto ni ikimasen.)
examples.en: I am not going to work today.

examples.native: 彼はお酒を飲まない。(kare wa osake o nomanai.)
時間がありません。(jikan ga arimasen.)
私は車を持っていません。(watashi wa kuruma o motte imasen.)
examples.en: I do not have a car.

commonMistakes: Forming the polite negative as ますない. Just change ます to ません.
Treating godan verbs ending in う as taking あ. 買う becomes 買わない, with わ.
Forgetting the irregular negative of ある is ない, not あらない.
Applying ichidan rules to godan verbs (飲む becomes 飲まない, not 飲ない).
Mixing the plain ない form with the polite ません in one sentence.
tip: Polite is the easy path: ます becomes ません. Save the plain ない form for casual speech and grammar chains.
